coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon quantum dots and silver nanoparticles: Application to mercuric ion sensing

Silver nanoparticles

Redox reaction
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the C-dots and Ag NPs
The FRET assembly of C-dots and Ag NPs
Optimization of the method using C-dots–Ag NPs assembly
Detection of mercuric ions in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FTIR (A), 1H NMR (B) and 13C NMR spectra (C) and XRD pattern (D) of the synthesized C-dots.
The TEM images (A) and zeta potential diagram (B) of the prepared C-dots.

The DLS zeta potential (A) and particle size distribution (B) of the synthesized Ag NPs obtained from DLS analysis, UV–Vis absorption spectra of the synthesized C-dots and Ag NPs (C), (inset: C-dots solution in water under visible (a) and UV (b) light, and lyophilized oily solid under visible (c) and UV (d) light), and the excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra of the C-dots (D).
The influence of pH (a), ionic strength (b), temperature (c) and time (d) on the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the C-dots in the absence and presence of different concentration of Ag NPs (A), effect of pH (B), buffer type and concentration (C) on the fluorescence intensity of FRET and redox systems, and (D) calibration curve for Hg(II) ion determination.
Optimization of some parameters affecting the quantum yield of the synthesized C-dots.
Results of Hg(II) ion detection in real samples along with the achieved relative recovery values for the spiked samples.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the proposed method with reported nanosensors for determination of Hg(II) ion.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer between carbon quantum dots and silver nanoparticles: Application to mercuric ion sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Spectroscopy Research Lab., Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, 35 Km Tabriz-Marageh Road, P.O. Box 53714-161, Tabriz 5375171379, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical Spectroscopy Research Lab., Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, 35 Km Tabriz-Marageh Road, P.O. Box 53714-161, Tabriz 5375171379, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of chemistry, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of chemistry, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Ardabil, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Schematic representation of the novel carbon quantum dots (CQD) synthesis and combination of a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) with a redox reaction to construct a turn-off/on fluorescent chemosensor for Hg(II) ion detection.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a non-radiative dipole-dipole coupling of two chromophores that leads to the energy transfer between the FRET pair. The FRET pair consists of a fluorophore and a quencher in close vicinity as an energy donor and acceptor, respectively, with different emission and excitation wavelengths. By absorbing a photon, the donor fluorophore is excited, and then the energy is transferred to the acceptor via a non-radiation pathway [1]. FRET process has been widely utilized as a unique, reliable and sensitive analytical method to determine different analytes [2–4]. Quantum dots [5–7], fluorescent proteins, organic dyes [8] and up-conversion fluorescence nanoparticles (UCNPs) [9] are some of fluorescent materials that have been applied to construct the FRET system through various strategies such as electrostatic attachment [10], covalent couple [11] and specific supramolecular interaction [12]. As a matter of fact, the FRET technique have been used to resolve significant issues in different fields.

Carbon dots (C-dots) are new, superior and universal member of fluorescent carbon materials with a diameter less than 10 nm. Highly tunable photoluminescence, up-conversion and electrochemiluminescence property, tiny size, excellent photostability, biocompatibility, easy functionalization with biomolecules, and chemical inertness are a number of key merits of C-dots [13]. Due to these properties, C-dots have been utilized as biosensors [14], gene transmission [15], drug carriers [16], and bioimaging probes [17]. As a result, the glowing carbon quantum dots (CQD) analytically exhibit a vast potential for different applications in chemistry. The C-dots could be applied as donors in FRET systems due to their fluorescent quenching property. On the other hand, owing to their high extinction coefficient and the broad absorption band, silver nanoparticles could be applied as efficient acceptors in FRET systems [18]. Recently, the FRET systems based on C-dots and silver nanoparticles have been reported for the determination of some analytes such as ochratoxin A and cysteine in different matrices [19,20]. However, there is no report on metal ions detection using the C-dots–silver nanoparticles FRET systems.

Concerns over toxic exposure to mercury have motivated the discovery of modern methods for monitoring aqueous Hg(II) ion. Kidney failure, vision impairment, deafness, brain damage, tremors are some of mercury toxicological effects. Industrial waste, gold mining, runoff, sewage and some of human activities are such sources releasing contamination to the ecosystem. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), 5.0 nmol L−1 is the permissible limit of mercury in drinking water. The demand for the facile and immediate determination of mercury ion in the environment and food products with high sensitivity and selectivity is the cause to search for optical feedback based upon fluorophores [21], chromophores [22], polymer [23], and noble metal-based probes [24]. Most of the proposed approaches thus far, are relied on the complexation of Hg(II) ion with different small molecules and its detection by instrumental techniques such as gas chromatography, absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectrometry [10,23–25]. Additionally, atomic absorption spectrometry [26], cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry [27], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [28], Rayleigh light-scattering [29] techniques have been applied for the quantification of Hg(II) ion.

The designed Hg(II) ion sensors, so far, have constructed based on photo-induced charge transfer, photo-induced electron transfer and FRET mechanisms and many of them have demonstrated quenching responses [21,30,31]. There are limited numbers of Hg(II) ion “turn-on” chemosensors on the foundation of FRET and none of them have constructed based on the redox system. Silver nanoparticles are the best selection for the desired FRET system which not only could absorb the non-radiative energy of the excited C-dots but also could react with the Hg(II) ions through redox system. Unlike bulk Ag which the standard reduction potential (E
° ) is comparable with that of Hg(II) ion, the E
° of silver nano-particles is lower than Hg(II) ion, which is the direct result of Ag size alteration [32,33]. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that the redox and FRET systems are combined to construct FRET-based Hg(II) ion chemosensor for the quantification of ultratrace amounts of Hg(II) ion. Furthermore, a novel carbon dots with a quantum yield of about 84% was synthesized using citric acid and dimethylglyoxim as precursors, and introduced to the major group of carbon-based quantum dots.

All reagents were of analytical grade, purchased from commercial sources and used without further treatment. Silver nitrate (AgNO3), mercury(II) nitrate (Hg(NO3)2), sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7), dimethylglyoxime (DMG, C4H8N2O2), citric acid (CA, C6H8O7), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), methanol and all salts were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). A stock solution of Hg(II) ion (500,000 nmol L−1) was prepared by dissolving the adequate amount of Hg(NO3)2 in 0.01 mol L−1 HCl and other desired concentration were obtained by its dilution with deionized water. All utilized glassware washed with a nitric acid solution (15% v/v), rinsed with deionized water and kept in oven for subsequent use.

The deionized water was obtained from a Millipore Milli-Q purification assembly (ρ = 18.2 MΩ.cm, 298 K). To record the fluorescence spectra and intensity measurements, a Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorophotometer, supplied with a xenon lamp as a light source, 1.0 cm quartz cell, photomultiplier (PMT) as a detector and LabSolutions RF software was utilized. All fluorescence spectra were recorded at 298 K with a scan rate of 600 nm min−1 and the emission slit width of 5 nm. The spectrophotometric measurements were carried out in the wavelength range of 200 to 800 nm with a double beam Shimadzu UV–Vis spectrophotometer using 1.0 cm quartz cells. To adjust the pH of the solutions, a Metrohm pH-meter model 632 (Herisau, Switzerland) was used. To characterize the synthesized C-dots, a NMR 250 MHz (Bruker, Germany), a Shimadzu Fourier transform infrared spectroscope model Prestige-2 (Kyoto, Japan), a Bruker X-ray diffractometer (XRD) model AXS (Germany), a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) model FEI TEC9G20 with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV, and a Horiba Scientific dynamic light scatter (DLS) model SZ-100 (Japan) were used.

To C-dots synthesis, first, 3.0 g CA was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water and sonicated in the ultrasonic bath for 15 min. Then, 1.1 g DMG was added into the 50 mL of the pure methanol and sonicated at the same time. After that, the attained solutions were mixed and sonicated for the extra 15 min. Finally, the colloidal solution was transferred into the 100 mL autoclave and kept in an oven at 453 K for 6 h. The pH of the obtained C-dots was adjusted to 7 by adding the concentrated sodium hydroxide solution and kept in a refrigerator at 277 K for further use.

The quantum yield was the criteria for the selection of C-dots. To optimize the efficiency of the as prepared product, some key parameters affecting the quantum yield of C-dots were investigated. By considering the constant amount of CA, different amounts of DMG (0.7–1.7 g) were tested and optimum value was found to be 1.1 g. Furthermore, the influence of heating temperature and time were individually evaluated in which the other conditions were kept constant. The consequences are shown in the Table 1 . To make sure whether any impurities are in the C-dots or not, thin layer chromatography (TLC) was applied prior to use. According to the TLC paper image, there is no any impact of initial material along with C-dots indicating only one product without any impurities has been synthesized.

The silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) were synthesized according to the previously reported procedure [34]. First, 100 mL of a mixture containing 0.25 mmol L−1 AgNO3 and 0.25 mmol L−1 sodium citrate solution was prepared. Then, 3 mL of 0.01 mol L−1 NaBH4 solution was added dropwise under vigorous stirring. A yellow colloidal solution of silver nanoparticles was generated during 20 min at ambient temperature. Finally, the mixture was undisturbedly remained for 8 h and kept in refrigerator for the further use. The concentration of the AgNPs was calculated to be 4.0 nmol L−1 using Mie-theory-based formula and Lambert-Beer's law [34].

The quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized C-dots in water was estimated in reference to quinine sulfate with the QY of 53% in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4 (n = 1.33) at 350 nm excitation, in which the applied excitation wavelengths and slit band widths were the same for the both samples [35]. The following equation was used to calculate QY of the C-dots.
(1)
QY =
QY ref
n 2
I
A ref
n ref 2
A
I ref
where QY, n, A and I are the quantum yield, refractive index of the solvent, absorbance and integrated fluorescence intensity of the compound, respectively. The subscript of ref is related to the reference compound. The absorbance values were kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm to minimize reabsorption effects. According to the above explanations, QY of 84% was found for the prepared C-dots.

The presented method was applied for the determination of Hg(II) ion in Shurabil lake water (Ardabil, Iran), industrial wastewater and tea samples. The wastewater sample was provided from industrial settlement 1 refinery (Ardabil, Iran) and filtered off through the cellulose acetate filter to remove the large particles. One of the most world popular beverages is tea and it contains low levels of heavy metals according to the previously repots [36–38]. Three kinds of tea samples were tested and all prepared as follows. 0.5 g of the grounded each tea sample was accurately weighted and placed in a beaker to be treated with concentrated HNO3 (65%) on the hotplate at 403 K till dryness. The resultant was transferred to a furnace and mineralized at 773 K during 1 h. The residue was dissolved in concentrated HCl (colorless solution) and the volume was made up to 10 mL with deionized water after pH adjustment (pH 4) [39]. To confirm the accuracy of the method, 100 mg of the certified reference material NIST SRM 1566b (Oyster Tissue) was digested using the concentrated nitric acid 65% (10 mL) and hydrogen peroxide 35% (5 mL) through mild heating to dryness according to our previous work [36]. After dissolution in diluted nitric acid (0.1 mol L−1) and adjustment of the pH at nearby 4, the final volume was reached to 10 mL with deionized water, and the concentration of Hg ions was measured following the general procedure.

An aliquot of 1 mL of the prepared Ag NPs was firstly poured into the vial containing 27 μL of the synthesized C-dots and the universal buffer solution (0.04 mol L−1, pH 4). Then, 1.4 mL of Hg(II) ion solution in the concentration range of 0.5–500.0 nmol L−1 was added into the solution and incubated for 40 min at room temperature. Finally, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at 400 nm with excitation at 350 nm. The entire volume of each sample was 3 mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the C-dots and Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared C-dots were structurally characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy and NMR. In the FT-IR analysis of C-dots, OH stretching vibrations are appeared at 3415 and 3242 cm−1 (Fig. 1A). The two peaks at 1710 and 1685 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of CO. The peaks related to the aromatic and aliphatic CH bonds are shown in 2956, 2924 and 2880 cm−1. Furthermore, the accessibility of CC (peak at 1573) and CO (peaks at 1276, 1213, 1028) are the obvious reasons for the existence of aromatic rings and ester groups, respectively, in the synthesized material. In the 1H NMR spectrum of the prepared C-dots, aliphatic hydrogens were detected between 1 and 4 ppm in which –CH3 group hydrogen and the aliphatic ring hydrogens were emerged in 1–1.5 and 2–4 ppm, respectively (Fig. 1B). Also, different –OH groups with different vicinities are demonstrated around 2–4 ppm. The region of 7–8.5 ppm is related to the aromatic hydrogens which is less noteworthy compared to aliphatic region, as a result the number of aliphatic rings in the structure are higher than aromatic ones and there is not any sign of carbocyclic acid hydrogens' in the region of 11–12 ppm. In the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. 1C), the appeared peak at 7–8, 20–50 and 55–90 ppm are related to –CH3, aliphatic ring carbons and attached carbon to hydroxyl groups, respectively. The sp2 aromatic rings carbons are shown in 120–140 ppm. Furthermore, two peaks at 150 and 157 ppm are demonstrated that two kinds of CN groups are available in the structure. The peaks between 165 and 180 ppm are the conformation of carbonyl group in the structure. In addition, the broad peak at around 2θ = 22° displayed in XRD pattern of the synthesized C-dots (Fig. 1D) suggests the highly disordered carbon atoms [40].

Fig. 2A demonstrates the TEM images of the C-dots, which confirms the particle diameters of 2–6 nm and uniform dispersion without obvious aggregation. The zeta potential measurements of the C-dots also declare the positive charge on the surface of the C-dots with the conductivity of 0.241 mS cm−1 with accordance to Fig. 2B. To stabilize the Ag NPs colloid, citrate is a proper candidate due to the creation of negative charge on the surface of Ag NPs. The DLS measurements of the as-prepared citrate stabilized Ag NPs demonstrate the nanoparticles are monodispersed in the average size of 37.8 nm with the negative surface charge conductivity of 0.209 mS cm−1, which are shown in Fig. 3A and B. On the other hand, the absorption peak of Ag NPs is overlapped with the emission spectrum of C-dots, that is the right choice for FRET system. Notably, the electrostatic interaction between the C-dots and Ag NPs ends up to the FRET mechanism. The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the Ag NPs and C-dots are shown in Fig. 3C. As can be seen from inside scheme of Fig. 3C, the C-dots solution is in light yellow and has no emission unless under UV lamp with the reflection of green light. Also, the freeze dried state of the C-dots is oily and emits the green light under the UV lamp. The excitation dependent fluorescence emission spectra of the C-dots in the excitation wavelength range of 220–420 nm are presented in Fig. 3D.

The photoluminescence stability of C-dots to the impacts of pH, temperature, dissolved oxygen, ionic strength was investigated as well as photobleaching and time effect. The fluorescence intensity of the C-dots remains constant at the pH range of 2–8 and decreases at higher pH values (Fig. 4a). Also, ionic strength of KCl salt with various concentration among 0–2 mol L−1 were tested on the fluorescence intensity and no significant change was made on it (Fig. 4b).

Temperature and dissolved oxygen are two important factors that intensely influence the photoluminescence intensity of a fluorophore. According to the experimental results, the decrement of C-dots fluorescence intensity in different regions of 293–303, 303–323 and 323–363 K are about 2, 12, 19%, respectively (Fig. 4c), and the fluorescence intensity is increased about 18% after deoxygenation for 15 min. The photochemical alteration of a fluorophore such that it permanently is unable to fluorescence is named photobleaching. The consequences show, the emission intensity of C-dots under the permanent radiation of UV lamp is constant till 180 min. It is worthy to mention that the synthesized C-dots are usable for four months without any decrease in intensity, and only 10% decreasing was occurred after 7 months, indicating the high stability of the synthesized carbon dots (Fig. 4d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FRET assembly of C-dots and Ag NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission intensity of the C-dots was monitored by adding different amounts of Ag NPs into the C-dots solution. As can be seen from Fig. 5A, the fluorescence intensity is gradually quenched at 440 nm, meanwhile, there is no fluorescence emission for Ag NPs. Hereupon, these ingredients are possibly assembled together via electrostatic interaction due to their opposite charge. Although some parameters such as pH, ionic strength and incubation time could directly affect the quenching, the optimization of Ag NPs concentration draw most of the attention to itself regarding the selection of appropriate amount of Ag NPs for Hg(II) ion reduction and construction of an off-on fluorescent nano-sensor for mercuric ion detection. Accordingly, the four equivalent concentration of colloidal Ag NPs solution was selected as the most potent concentration for FRET and redox process among the 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 5, and 6 times concentration of the synthesized Ag NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the method using C-dots–Ag NPs assembly
ce:section.ce:para: To measure mercuric ions through the proposed assay, some parameters affecting the efficiency of the method for instance, the concentration of C-dots, pH, ionic strength and incubation time were investigated and optimized. Both designed FRET and redox reaction were straightly influenced by the above mentioned factors. Firstly, various concentrations of C-dots through the rage from 0.07 to 0.34 mg mL−1 were prepared and 0.22 mg mL−1 was selected due to the providing the highest fluorescence intensity (data are not shown).

As the stability of the components is influenced by the pH, the efficiency of two procedures were then evaluated, as according to Fig. 5B. In one hand, the emission intensity of C-dots–Ag NPs assembly is decreased until pH 4 and remained constant till pH 8, while the reduction process of Hg(II) ions to Hg0 is strongly diminished through the movement from acidic to alkaline media. On the other hand, the maximum fluorescence intensity of C-dots was observed in the pH range of 2–8, and at higher pH it was sharply declined. In acidic media, possibly, the surface hydroxyl groups of C-dots are in the form of OH2 + and some negative charge of the citrate stabilized Ag NPs are neutralized. However, the remainder negative charge of the C-dots is enough to construct FRET through electrostatic interaction with C-dots. Furthermore, mercuric hydroxo complexes are formed at higher pH values which might not interact with the surface of Ag NPs [32]. As a result, pH 4 was selected for the subsequent experiments due to the best formation of FRET and redox reaction.

The influence of ionic strength on the suggested process was evaluated by applying three concentrations of two kinds of buffer solutions. Universal and phthalate buffer solutions are made up by citric acid-boric acid-phosphoric acid and potassium hydrogen phthalate, respectively, in which the pH is adjusted by different volumes of NaOH solution at 4. As shown in Fig. 5C, the interaction of potassium hydrogen phthalate with FRET components is stronger, and the increase in the concentration of buffer solution from 0.04 to 0.1 mol L−1 has no significant effect on the fluorescence intensity. Consequently, 0.04 mol L−1 of the universal buffer solution (pH 4) was utilized for the further experiments.

Incubation time is the other significant parameter which must be taken into account to completely quench the C-dots emission during FRET and accomplish the reduction of mercuric ions. Based on the acquired results, the incubation time had no impact on FRET system. In comparison, the desired redox reaction was in the necessity of time and completed during 40 min, and the extra time had no effect on the process (data are not shown). Hence, 40 min was selected as an optimum incubation time for the rest of the experiments.

The fluorescence spectra of the C-dots–Ag NPs in the presence of various concentrations of Hg(II) ion were evaluated under the above-mentioned optimal conditions. Remarkably, the FRET-decreased fluorescence intensity of the C-dots recovered with the gradual increase of mercuric ions concentrations. The relationship between Hg(II) ion concentration and (F − F0)/F0 was investigated in the range between 0.5 and 1200.0 nμmol L−1, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of Hg(II) ion, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5D, the calibration plot is linear in the concentration range of 0.5–500.0 nmol L−1 with a regression equation of (F − F0)/F0 = 0.0154 C
Hg(II) + 1.0602 and the determination coefficient (R
2 ) of 0.9965, where C
Hg(II) illustrates the Hg(II) ion concentration (nmol L−1). The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification were found to be 0.10 and 0.35 nmol L−1, according to 3S
b /m and 10 S
b /m, respectively. The obtained LOD is also quite competitive with respect to the previous reported articles and it is far below the regulated safety limits by WHO for drinking water. To investigate the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the method, five replicates of the same experiments were applied for different concentrations of Hg(II) ion and the measured amounts confirm the good repeatability of the method (%RSD = 0.5–2.6).

To investigate the selectivity of the proposed approach, 50 and 100-fold concentrations of the potent interfering substances such as Cd2+, Ni2+, pb2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Na+, and Mg2+ ions were added into 250 nmol L−1 of Hg(II) ion. 100-fold higher concentration of Ni2+, pb2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+ and 50-fold higher concentration of Zn2+, Cr3+, Cd2+ alter the fluorescence signal less than ±5%, hence no considerable interference was occurred upon Hg(II) ion coexisting. As a result, the proposed method could be applied as the practical and promising approach for Hg(II) ion determination. The inset picture of Fig. 5D reflects the selectivity of the method toward Hg(II) ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of mercuric ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The FRET based assay of mercuric ion were applied in lake water and wastewater samples. In addition, green tea and two kinds of black tea (Galin and Lahijan) samples were analyzed. It can be seen from Table 2 that all analyzed real samples contain different levels of Hg(II) ion and it was obvious regarding previous works [36]. To evaluate the practicality and efficiency of the method, different concentrations of mercuric ion were spiked into the real samples and the procedure was carried out as according to “General procedure” section. Finally, the measured amounts were compared with the spiked ones and the relative recovery values between 98.0 and 104.0% revealed that the suggested turn-off/on fluorescent sensor based on FRET-redox system could reliably utilize to determine Hg(II) ion in complex matrices. In addition, the accuracy of the method was assessed by analyzing of the certified reference material NIST SRM 1566b. The acquired value for Hg(II) ion was 36.7 ± 1.1 ng g−1 (mean of three determinations ± standard deviation), which is in good agreement with the certified value of the analyte (37.1 ± 1.3 ng g−1). So, according to the Student t-test, there is no significant difference between the certified and assayed values at 95% confidence level, indicating the high accuracy of the method.

Table 3 compares several analytical features of the suggested method with some reported sensors. According to these data, the detection limit of the presented method is better than other reported methods with the exception of Ref. [41]. In addition, the linear dynamic range and the relative standard deviation of the method are comparable or better than other reported methods. It should be mentioned that, neither silver nanoparticles [36] nor carbon nanoparticles [42] are not solely capable for Hg(II) ion detection in such range of linearity and detection limit. Moreover, in comparison with nanogold or modified nanogold [29,41,43], the synthesis of the used carbon dots and Ag nanoparticles is novel, fast and inexpensive. Also, the presented method could be successfully applied for the determination of mercuric ions in water and tea samples (Table 2) however, other methods have just been conducted on water samples analysis.

In this research, a novel method based on the combination of a fluorescence resonance energy transfer process with a redox reaction was introduced for trace determination of mercuric ion. The applied fluorophore in the FRET system belonging to the vast group of carbon quantum dots was synthesized for the first time. The synthesized carbon quantum dots had the highest fluorescence emission intensity with the quantum yield of 84%. Silver nanoparticles play a significant role in the FRET assembly as the acceptor, meanwhile, they perform as potent nanomaterials for the measurements of mercuric ions. By adding Hg(II) ion, the FRET-diminished fluorescence intensity of the combined C-dots-Ag NPs system was recovered via oxidation of the Ag NPs by Hg(II) ions. Consequently, a novel turn-off/on fluorescent chemosensor was constructed for Hg(II) ion detection as there was a linear relationship between the recovered fluorescence intensity and Hg(II) ion concentration in the range of 0.5–500.0 nmol L−1. The method was applied for Hg(II) ion determination in wastewater, lake water, green tea and black tea samples and satisfactory relative recovery values (98.0–104.0%) were attained in the case of the spiked samples. All in all, the developed FRET-redox fluorescent nanosensor can utilize for the quantification of Hg(II) ion in real samples with proper selectivity and sensitivity.

Hossein Abdolmohammad-Zadeh: Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Investigation. Zhila Azari: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Investigation, Validation, Software, Visualization, Data curation. Eslam Pourbasheer: Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Research Council of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University (Grant no. ASMU/98372–19) for financial support of this project.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The construction of a FRET assembly by using gold nanoclusters and carbon dots and their application as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yu, C.X. Zhang, L. Zhang, Y.R. Xue, H.W. Li, Y. Wu, The construction of a FRET assembly by using gold nanoclusters and carbon dots and their application as a ratiometric probe for cysteine detection, Sensor. Actuat. B: Chem. 263 (2018) 327–335.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorometric determination of the antibiotic kanamycin by aptamer-induced FRET quenching and recovery between MoS2 nanosheets and carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, T. Ma, S. Ma, Y. Liu, Y. Tian, R. Wang, Y. Jiang, D. Hou, J. Wang, Fluorometric determination of the antibiotic kanamycin by aptamer-induced FRET quenching and recovery between MoS2 nanosheets and carbon dots, Microchim. Acta 184 (2017) 203–210.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: FRET-Förster Resonance Energy Transfer: From Theory to Applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.L. Medintz, N. Hildebrandt, FRET-Förster resonance energy transfer: from theory to applications, John Wiley & Sons (2013).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in FRET for the study of protein interactions and dynamics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Okamoto, Y. Sako, Recent advances in FRET for the study of protein interactions and dynamics, Curr. Opin. Struct. Biol. 46 (2017) 16–23.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and application of quantum dots FRET-based protease sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Shi, V. De Paoli, N. Rosenzweig, Z. Rosenzweig, Synthesis and application of quantum dots FRET-based protease sensors, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 128 (2006) 10378–10379.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous detection of pathogenic bacteria using an aptamer based biosensor and dual fluorescence resonance energy transfer from quantum dots to carbon nanoparticles

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Duan, S. Wu, S. Dai, T. Miao, J. Chen, Z. Wang, Simultaneous detection of pathogenic bacteria using an aptamer based biosensor and dual fluorescence resonance energy transfer from quantum dots to carbon nanoparticles, Microchim. Acta 182 (2015) 917–923.
